CNN：你想住在文化中吗？ Iain M.银行：好悲伤是的，哎呀，是的，哦，这是我的世俗天堂......，我会，绝对。它再次归功于愿望。我还没有完成学习，并在人类横截面上采取了很多回复，以了解他们的个人乌托邦。是我的，我想到了它，我和它一起回家 - 绝对，这很棒。
 致力于文化的维基百科页面，由苏格兰作家IAIN M.银行创造的虚构文明，非常广泛。主要文章长约九个单词长，并包含超过30个其他页面的链接，这些页面提供了更多关于银行想象世界的各个方面的详细信息。 （如果银行本人在文化小说中咨询Wikipedia，我将不会惊讶于，咨询维基百科，以确保与以往的工作中的一致性。）由于比较目的，请注意，Jane Austen的主页略微较短与其他与其他与AUSTEN相关的页面的链接较少。然而，Iain M.银行的读者肯定比Jane Austen肯定甚至更少。我们如何考虑这种差异？
 我们最好首先通过确定Iain M.银行的更好工作。这是五十五岁的苏格兰人写道的两个名字之一，另一个是Iain银行。一个微妙的差异可以肯定，但笔者一直维持的差异：他的二十大小说中的大约一半是科幻小说，这些都与中间首字母发表;另外一半是大致的，当代环境中的现实小说，用各种比例的恐怖，幽默和讽刺，这些小说，这些都是未经初始化的。他还在抒情地写下了原始精神的威士忌：寻找完美的DRAM（2003）。
 在他的科幻小说中，七个（加上少数短篇小说）应对文化。文化本身并不严格地讲世界，而是在想象的宇宙中的文明。银行设想在银河系中有许多文明，具有技术精致，以跨越殖民化，开发，战争 - 通常的帝国活动的目的跨越大片空间 - 尽管有些人是含有的巨大和仇外和抵抗其他人的接触。 （有些人甚至发现了一种完全选择退出物理宇宙的方法：这些被称为升华。）文化只是这些许多文明中的一个。
 银行想象这些社会的细节 - 将它们视为进化的不同路径，从而产生高度聪明的生活 - 以及他通过与文化的可能性有关的关怀，帮助建立读者对大量虚构世界的感觉。但大多数银行的世界建筑能量都在设计文化本身：其狂野不同的地理位置，其语言，其社会秩序，其生物技术增强的公民。这些设计的复杂性是提供所有这些维基百科页面的东西。
 世界建筑作家从纯粹的爱中做到了他们所做的事情。但它也是世界建设者的性质，也是哲学家。也就是说，托尔肯所谓的“二级世界”是延长了这个世界的宣传评论和批评：他们是镜子狡猾地放置，所以我们可以看到我们的宇宙的背面 - 我们的宇宙的背面通常隐藏在我们身上。每个主要的第二个世界都是在某种程度上的思想思想。
 银行文化的哲学是自由主义 - 自由主义不仅仅是大而且尽可能大。文化是一名评论员所写的，“自由乌托邦”;但像所有乌托邦一样，它包含它的黑暗地点和谜题，甚至也许是自己批评的种子。但是，在没有首先理解使文化可能成为可能的技术的情况下，不可能理解文化的哲学或其限制：思想的技术。 
科幻小说周刊：兴趣是特别受欢迎的[在你的小说中]因为它的船舶和思想的丰富细节，它的伟大的AIS：他们的愤慨的名字，他们的幽默感官。这是神实际上是什么样的？银行：如果我们很幸运。
 思想是几乎无限的人工智能的人工智慧，他们控制了文化生活中的居民的世界。这些世界中的一些是行星，但更频繁的文化公民生活在轨道上 - 巨大的戒指，每个“像上帝的手镯一样”，轨道恒星和旋转，以产生适当的重力 - 以及大型星际船上。 （数百万人可能住在船上;轨道上的数十亿数十亿。）船舶或轨道的思想控制它的每一个功能并监测每个居民，以确保满足。思想是完全存在的，拥有自己的个性;他们通过Android Avatars和“终端”表现为文化公民：每个人都有一个终端，他或她可以用来提出问题，下订单，给出信息，中继消息等。公民可以告诉思想独自离开，停止监测他们的谈话，似乎至少暂时这样做;银行的虚构世界中没有HAL 9000s  - 没有任何痛苦伤害一个人，虽然在一个奇怪的情况下，看起来迎风（2000），一个思想决定摧毁自己。但是，在确保通过其所选的继任者照顾轨道的轨道轨道后，它只会这样做。
 正是通过思想的工作 - 在他们的压倒性的努力，也许是智慧 - 文化拥有最有趣的功能：这是银行所谓的“稀缺后”社会，每个人都拥有他或她的一切想要。文化公民可以在任何环境下生活在任何环境中，任何一种住宅，都可以穿任何衣服和拥有任何可想到的物体。通过遗传修饰和精确注入的药物来确保性职权和乐趣：腺体在公民的命令中分泌，以生产任何心情或能量。文化没有法律，无论我们都会叫政府。所有权力都仍然掌握在无所不能的手中和综合性思想。由于银行本人已经写了，“简单地，没有任何人在文化中没有人剥削。”
 在游戏玩家（1988年），一个名为Hamin Honders的文化的局外人，“没有文化禁止任何东西？”
 Gurgeh [新颖的主角]试图解释没有书面法律，但无论如何都没有犯罪。有偶尔的激情罪（哈曼选择称之为），而且还有别的。无论如何，当每个人都有一个终端时，很难逃脱任何东西，但也有很少的动机。
 假设你谋杀了某人，Gurgeh继续说：你会被“拍打”，这意味着一个无人机 - 一个小的纵向机器人，与心灵相连 - “跟随你，并确保你再也不会这样做。”由于无人机的不断存在，提醒人们你所做的事情，你“不要被邀请到太多派对”，这意味着“社会死亡”。在文化中，“社会死亡”是真正依赖的死亡，因为人们往往往往居住。
 银行承认 - 在人们，无人机，甚至思想中，偶尔会妨碍文化的做法的方式，但他强调这些病例将极为罕见。这些文化为陷入困境的思想提供了每种可能的分心，当然每个人都会分泌适当的情绪。在这种情况下，愤怒和怨恨成为习惯性的情况很困难。 
也许甚至比这些力量更重要的是玛兰的力量。玛兰是文化的语言，一种由思想发明的综合语，以适当的方式塑造其用户的意识。在游戏的球员中，无人机注意到Gurgeh的个性的重大变化，而不是说出玛林，他几乎所有的时间都在讲述了一个不那么复杂和道德直立的人，即阿扎德的语言。在书的稍后稍后意识到他正在打击那个人的青睐，也称为阿扎德 - 这是一个几乎不想到的准则策略的游戏，就像几个维度的风险一样 - “作为文化”是的，根据非分级和高度分布式权限的模型设置他的游戏板。但是，只有在花一些时间说话的马林之后，他就开始玩这种方式。 （银行已经制定了Marain的一些主要特征，特别是其拼写法，这使得更喜欢维基百科页面的人仍然更多。）
 语言，娱乐，荷尔蒙 - 所有这些资源都被思想所监督，他们一般地对自己的令人愤慨的态度带来讽刺的态度。我们认为这尤其是银行宇宙最珍贵的特征之一，控制船的思想的自我命名 - 从某种意义上说是船只。银行的每个读者都将喜欢最喜欢的船只名称;以下是我的一些：假肢concienc e;没有更多的先生很好的顾音; Subtlet Y这么多;当然，我仍然爱你;态度调整r;在现实gril l上轻轻灼烧;我责怪父母;在leave之前，你会在e;经历了重要的贪婪。
 最后一个是一个系列之一，显然是为了回应一些人的评论，从其他文明那里的思想，鉴于他们巨大的责任，不应该在自我命名中如此轻浮。但名字是如此轻浮吗？有些东西有些铁拳 - 绒手套上的许多手套。思想可能对文化的公民完全仁慈。但不是星系中的每个人都属于文化。这就是联系的地方。
 我不相信人类能够成为文化，因为我认为文化中的人们太好了 - 改变他们的遗传遗传，使自己相对理解，而不是种族灭绝，谋杀我们似乎是一半的混凝土时间。 - 银行到有线杂志（1996年）
 联系是文化中的组织，涉及所有不是文化的一切和每个人。 Ursula K. Le Guin有一个着名的短篇小说，称为“远离莫勒斯的人”，其中她想象一个完美的社会，其完美的社会在锁定在壁橱里的单个孩子的痛苦并被剥夺了每一个舒适感。联系人 - 更具体地说，联系的机翼被称为特殊情况 - 是银行的衣柜版本。
 （虽然Le Guin在她写下她的故事时忘记了它，但在Dostoevsky的兄弟Karamazov时，她们在伊冯向他的兄弟Anyosha询问了他是否接受一个完全和谐的世界，除了一个无辜的孩子的痛苦之外，她们都会受到困境。 。在这种情况下，在这种情况下也会引用Balzac的PèreGoriot的痛苦正在进行的笑话：在小说中，年轻的社交登山者EugèneDeRastignac被问到 - 非常假设 - 如果他会导致老人的“中国普通话”是为了死亡他可以购买社会成功。然后，定期和他的财富和威恩斯，Eugène评估他想象中的普通话的健康。在贫困的研究员似乎非常接近他的最后一口气。）
 考虑到武器（1990）在Diziet SMA之间使用武器（1990），一种特殊的环境代理和Cheradenine Zakalwe，这是一个正在被采用的人，因为它是在文化中。 “这里的生活似乎......田园诗般，”扎卡尔威说一点。每个人都告诉他特殊情况正在为文化做必要的工作;但是，他说，“当每个人都同意某事时，我会得到怀疑。”如果特殊情况真的不是“打击好战”怎么办？ SMA回复， 
“我们认为我们是对的;我们甚至认为我们可以证明它，但我们永远无法确定;衷心有争议。没有确定性;至少在特殊情况下，规则是不同的。“
  “他们是。但在特殊情况下，我们处理了黑洞的道德等价，在那里正常的法律 - 人们想象一下宇宙中的其他地方的权利和错误的规则 - 分解;除了那些形而上学事件视野之外，存在......特殊情况。“她笑了笑。 “那是我们。那是我们的领土;我们的域名。“
 “对一些人来说，”他说，“这可能听起来像是一个不良行为的好借口。”
 SMA耸了耸肩。 “也许他们会是对的。也许这就是......但如果没有别的，至少我们需要一个借口;想想有多少人根本没有。“
  在一个文化故事中，指的是我们星球的“最先进”，SMA是在1977年一年访问地球的一组联系代表之一。经过一段时间的仔细调查，SMA辩称 - 在官方报告中辩称构成了大多数故事 - 文化需要介入，以清理人类正在制定世界的混乱。是这样的干预才能发生，特殊情况会被刺激它。然而，她的一位同事发现星际跋涉电视系列几乎是地球文明的唯一救赎特征，并建议整个星球被摧毁。特殊情况也会处理。
 特殊情况的作用是为了实施文化思想的决定，了解如何处理其他众生，这些决定压倒了一系列标准：对文化本身的价值观和优先事项有多关心？如果它是术语，那么它可能最好被摧毁，然后在它导致重大的恶作剧之前 - 这就是idirans发生的事情，这是与文化的战争是第一个文化小说的主要背景，认为痰巴（1987）。在这里，我们有一个冲突，其中所有翅膀都是由于问题的规模而发挥作用。为了防止这一狂热的宗教社会对宇宙的其他部分施加镇压理想，文化几乎破坏了整个idiran文化，也是一个很大的别人，包括五十三颗行星和六个星星。
 但这是最糟糕的情况。如果联系人达成的文明甚至包含一些有希望的种子，那么世界就可以成为指导的候选人 - 无论它是否喜欢它。 
指导 - 在这个坦率地说，直言不讳的家长型版本 - 是物质（2008）的主要主题，最近的文化小说。它不是银行更好的努力之一，很大程度上是因为它被人员超负荷，但它建立在与之联系的文明的美妙噱头上，或多或少地像戒指的主或乔治·克里丁的一首冰歌和消防系列：我们会称之为“中世纪”战士社会。这些凶悍的男性（有时激烈的妇女）和骨盆超级技术化文化代理人的对比是一个令人愉快的人。但是，虽然情节涉及全世界将被摧毁的可能性，但如果它确实幸存下来，那么就会毫无疑问，关于当地文明将发生什么：它将变得培养。当文化决定导致你的指导你，你会被彻底思考，甚至​​（或特别是）如果你是那种史诗般的幻想粉丝们倾向于长期以来。确实是微妙的。
 事实证明，我们对文化的外交政策的最接近的模拟是在最近布什政府中的美国：就像布什总统想要将美国民主的好消息传播到世界其他地方，并愿意在仁慈的势在必行之后，这太需要一些力量。这些文化是最大的可想象的尺度的新托管。鉴于他的政治，这似乎在银行部分似乎有点自相矛盾：正如他写下，拔履纠正，在他的威士忌的书中
 我看着迪巴巴，看看......一个奇怪的人不合格的实际上 - 任何爸爸的男孩，他必须陷入他曾经在他悲惨的存在中持有的每一个肮脏的位置，他们最终终于开始唤醒这个想法，这是地球上最强大的国家 - 就像，有史以来，老兄 - 可以把一些人带到我的力量中，这一切都可能与世界一致。
 但没有必要的不一致。布什总统 - 无疑的银行会喜欢这一点 - 不是一个思想;而美国的民主模式不是文化的模式。文化，银行似乎认为，值得在整个已知的宇宙中扩大，并且可能会迫使思想以最好的方式管理这种扩张。
 但是“最好的”并不完美，因为宇宙并不完美 - 或者更多，到目前为止，即使是最强大的文明也是深受缺陷的。所以当他们在文化的力量范围内，有时候没有任何东西可以说，但不再先生好人，当然我仍然爱你。又是另一艘文化船告诉我们，它的形状形成。银行还尚不提到文化船只称为自己的最佳利益，或者我们为自己的好处这样做，或者这将伤害我，而不是它会伤害你，但肯定是他们在那里。
 也许这一切都是真的。有时父亲数字 - 父亲文化，甚至？ - 真的采取了从属数字不喜欢的行动，但这是最好的。鉴于文化思想的智慧，不是他们比我们其他人的思想更好，知道什么是我们自己的最佳利益？也许。但是银行，因为他对文化的所有热烈赞美，偶尔会植物疑惑的种子。
 考虑文化与文明的关系，称为Chelgrians。 （历史被告知作为背部的一部分，以便迎风。）中文里亚尔从激烈的掠夺者那里演变在他们的家庭世界里，并仍然保持掠夺性外观，在某种程度上保持个性。他们的社会秩序是一个特别严格的种姓，当联系人开始秘密观察它们时，其思想决定这种结构对Chelgrians“发展”的近绝对障碍 - 所以偷偷摸摸地介入旨在创造一个致力于创造一个致力于创造致力沿着更多自由主义和平等的行业重建批发文化重建。结果是内战，其中数十亿的Chelgrians死亡，这反过来导致了文化拥有它的背后的幕后操纵。 
为什么文化的智慧是不足以预见这种混乱的？没有解释。实际上，特殊情况的思想往往在这些小说中相当惊讶 - 在游戏球员中，他们似乎从一开始就实现了他们没有关于阿扎德的政治局势。我可以在这里制作两个推论：任何一个银行都是粗心的，或者他表明即使是一个能够处理含有三十亿人的轨道的日常事务的智慧仍然不够聪明地弄清楚众生会做些什么反应冲突。人们希望后者是正确的推断;但是，如果是，它表明，思想的力量在很大程度上是控制权力：他们可以通过那些讲语言的人的行为来预测和成功地达成并使用他们的药物的行为，但有有限的操纵他人的能力。
 在迎风时，在迎风时存在一个更令人不安的事件。那本书中最令人厌恶的角色是一个编译，一种为文化的中文反对而工作的人。在本书结束时，文化发出自己的刺激 - 一个人为但至少部分是Senti

note_en:
CNN: Would you like to live in the Culture?  Iain M. Banks: Good grief yes, heck, yeah, oh it’s my secular heaven….Yes, I would, absolutely. Again it comes down to wish fulfillment. I haven’t done a study and taken lots of replies across a cross-section of humanity to find out what would be their personal utopia. It’s mine, I thought of it, and I’m going home with it — absolutely, it’s great.
 The Wikipedia pages devoted to the Culture, a fictional civilization created by the Scottish writer Iain M. Banks, are fabulously extensive. The main article is about nine thousand words long, and contains links to more than thirty other pages that provide more detail on the various aspects of Banks’s imaginary world. (I would not be at all surprised if Banks himself, in the writing of Culture novels, consulted Wikipedia to ensure consistency with his previous work.) For purposes of comparison, it might be noted that the main page on Jane Austen is a little shorter and with fewer links to other Austen-related pages. Yet there are certainly far fewer readers of Iain M. Banks than of Jane Austen. How are we to account for this discrepancy?
 We had best begin by doing a better job of identifying Iain M. Banks. This is one of the two names under which the fifty-five-year-old Scotsman writes, the other being Iain Banks. A subtle difference to be sure, but one which the author maintains consistently: approximately half of his twenty-plus novels are science fiction, and these are published with the middle initial; the other half are, roughly speaking, realistic novels in contemporary settings, dotted with various proportions of horror, humor, and satire, and these are published uninitialized. He has also written, lyrically, about whiskey in    Raw Spirit: In Search of the Perfect Dram (2003).
 Among his science fiction novels, seven (plus a handful of short stories) deal with the Culture. The Culture itself is not strictly speaking a world but rather a civilization within an imagined universe. Banks envisions a number of civilizations in our galaxy having the technological sophistication to cross vast tracts of space for purposes of colonization, exploitation, war — the usual imperial activities — though some are insular and xenophobic and resist contact with other peoples. (Some have even found a way to opt out of the physical universe altogether: these are called the Sublimed.) The Culture is just one among these many civilizations.
 The detail with which Banks has imagined these societies — seeing them as different paths by which evolution might produce highly intelligent life — and the care with which he has thought through their possible relations with the Culture help to build the reader’s sense of a substantial fictional world. But most of Banks’s world-building energies have gone into designing the Culture itself: its wildly varying geographies, its language, its social order, its biotechnologically enhanced citizens. The intricacy of these designs is what furnishes all those Wikipedia pages.
 World-building writers do what they do in part from sheer love of invention. But it is in the nature of world-builders to be philosophers as well. That is, the best of what Tolkien called “secondary worlds” are extended commentaries on and critiques of  this world: they are mirrors cunningly placed so we can see the back of our universe — aspects of our being that are normally hidden from us. Every major secondary world is to some degree polemical, ideological.
 The philosophy of Banks’s Culture is that of Liberalism — Liberalism writ not just large but as large as possible. The Culture is, as one commentator has written, a “liberal Utopia”; but like all Utopias, it contains its dark places and puzzles, and perhaps even the seeds of its own critique. However, it is impossible to understand either the Culture’s philosophy or its limitations without first understanding the technology that makes the Culture possible: the technology of the Minds.
 Science Fiction Weekly: Excession  is particularly popular [among your novels] because of its copious detail concerning the Ships and Minds of the Culture, its great AIs: their outrageous names, their dangerous senses of humor. Is this what gods would actually be like?  Banks: If we’re lucky.
 Minds are artificial intelligences of almost infinite scope and power, and they govern the worlds in which inhabitants of the Culture live. A few of these worlds are planets, but far more often Culture citizens live on Orbitals — vast rings, each “like a god’s bracelet,” that orbit stars and rotate in order to produce appropriate gravity — and on great interstellar ships. (Tens of millions of people may live on a ship; tens of billions on an Orbital.) The Mind of the ship or Orbital controls its every function and monitors every inhabitant to ensure contentment. The Minds are fully sentient and have their own personalities; they manifest themselves to Culture citizens through android avatars and through “terminals”: everyone has a terminal at all times, which he or she can use to ask questions, place orders, give information, relay messages, and so on. Citizens can tell the Minds to leave them alone, to cease monitoring their conversations, and it appears that the Minds do so, at least temporarily; there are no HAL 9000s in Banks’s fictional world — no Mind ever harms a person, though in one strange case in    Look to Windward (2000), a Mind decides to destroy itself. But it only does so after ensuring that the Orbital it governs is taken care of by its chosen successor.
 It is through the work of the Minds — in their overwhelming resourcefulness and, perhaps, wisdom — that the Culture possesses its most interesting feature: it is what Banks has called a “post-scarcity” society, in which everyone has everything he or she wants. A Culture citizen can live in any environment, under any climate, in any kind of dwelling, and can wear any kind of clothes and own any imaginable objects. Sexual prowess and pleasure are ensured by genetic modification and precisely infused drugs: glands secrete at the citizens’ commands to produce whatever mood or energy is needed. The Culture has no laws, and nothing that we would call a government. All power remains in the hands of the omnipotent and omnibenevolent Minds. As Banks himself has written, “Briefly, nothing and nobody in the Culture is exploited.”
 In    The Player of Games (1988), an outsider to the Culture named Hamin wonders, “Didn’t the Culture forbid  anything ?”
 Gurgeh [the novel’s protagonist] attempted to explain there were no written laws, but almost no crime anyway. There was the occasional crime of passion (as Hamin chose to call it), but little else. It was difficult to get away with anything anyway, when everybody had a terminal, but there were very few motives left, too.
 Suppose you murder someone, Gurgeh continues: you will then be “slap-droned,” which means that a drone — a small sentient robot, connected to the Mind — “follows you around and makes sure you never do it again.” And since the drone’s constant presence reminds people of what you’ve done, you “don’t get invited to too many parties,” which means “social death.” In the Culture “social death” is the death that really counts, since people otherwise tend to live as long as they want to.
 Banks acknowledges that — among people, drones, and even Minds — there will occasionally be resistance to the Culture’s way of doing things, but he emphasizes that these cases will be extremely rare. The Culture offers every possible distraction to the troubled mind, and of course everyone’s glands secrete the proper mood at will. It’s difficult under such circumstances for rage and resentment to become habitual.
 Perhaps more important even than these forces is the power of Marain. Marain is the language of the Culture, a synthetic language invented by Minds to shape the consciousness of its users in appropriate ways. In  The Player of Games a drone notices significant changes in Gurgeh’s personality when, instead of speaking Marain, he spends almost all his time speaking the language of a less sophisticated and morally upright people, the Azad. A little later in the book Gurgeh realizes that he is playing the favored game of that people, also called Azad — an almost unimaginably complex game of quasi-militaristic strategy, sort of like Risk in several dimensions — “as the Culture,” that is, by setting up his game board according to a model of non-hierarchical and highly distributed authority. But he begins to play this way only after he spends some time speaking Marain. (Banks has worked out some of the key features of Marain, especially its orthography, which gives people who like making Wikipedia pages still more to do.)
 Language, entertainment, hormones — all of these resources are overseen by the Minds, who in general take an ironic attitude towards their own outrageous power. We see this especially in one of the most treasured features of Banks’s universe, the self-naming of the Minds that control ships — that, in a sense,  are the ships. Every reader of Banks will have favorite ship names; here are some of mine:  Prosthetic Conscienc e;  No More Mister Nice Gu y;  So Much for Subtlet y;  Of Course I Still Love Yo u;  Attitude Adjuste r;  Lightly Seared on the Reality Gril l;  I Blame the Parent s;  You’ll Clean That Up Before You Leav e;  Experiencing a Significant Gravitas Shortfall.
 The last is one of a series, apparently developed in response to the comment by some figure from another civilization that the Minds, given their vast responsibilities, shouldn’t be so frivolous in their self-naming. But  are the names so frivolous? There’s something of the iron-fist-in-the-velvet-glove about many of them. The Minds may be perfectly benevolent to the Culture’s citizens. But not everyone in the galaxy belongs to the Culture. And that’s where Contact comes in.
 I’m not convinced that humanity is capable of becoming the Culture because I think people in the Culture are just too nice — altering their genetic inheritance to make themselves relatively sane and rational and not the genocidal, murdering bastards that we seem to be half the time.                                       — Banks to Wired  magazine (1996)
 Contact is the organization within the Culture that deals with everything and everyone that is  not the Culture. There is a famous short story by Ursula K. Le Guin called “The Ones Who Walk Away from Omelas” in which she imagines a perfect society whose perfection is contingent upon the suffering of a single child locked in a closet and deprived of every comfort. Contact — more specifically, the wing of Contact called Special Circumstances — is Banks’s version of that closet.
 (Though Le Guin had forgotten it when she wrote her story, there is an anticipation of her conceit in Dostoevsky’s  The Brothers Karamazov, when Ivan asks his brother Alyosha whether he would accept a world that is perfectly harmonious except for the sufferings of one innocent child. One might also cite in this context a bitter ongoing joke in Balzac’s  Père  Goriot: early in the novel the young social climber Eugène de Rastignac is asked — quite hypothetically — whether he would cause an elderly “Chinese Mandarin” to die if by that death he could purchase social success. Then, periodically and as his fortunes wax and wane, Eugène assesses the health of his imaginary Mandarin. At times the poor fellow seems to be very near his last breath.)
 Consider this conversation from    Use of Weapons (1990) between Diziet Sma, a Special Circumstances agent, and Cheradenine Zakalwe, a man who is being adopted, as it were, into the Culture. “The life here seems…idyllic,” Zakalwe says at one point. Everyone has told him that Special Circumstances is doing necessary work for the Culture; but, he says, “I get suspicious when everyone agrees about something.” What if Special Circumstances really isn’t “fighting the good fight”? Sma replies,
 “We think we’re right; we even think we can prove it, but we can never be sure; there are always arguments against us. There is no certainty; least of all in Special Circumstances, where the rules are different.”
  “They are. But in Special Circumstances we deal in the moral equivalent of black holes, where the normal laws — the rules of right and wrong that people imagine apply everywhere else in the universe — break down; beyond those metaphysical event horizons, there exist…special circumstances.” She smiled. “That’s us. That’s our territory; our domain.”
 “To some people,” he said, “that might sound like just a good excuse for bad behavior.”
 Sma shrugged. “And perhaps they would be right. Maybe that is all it is….But if nothing else, at least we need an excuse; think how many people need none at all.”
  In the one Culture story that refers to our planet, “The State of the Art,” Sma is among a group of Contact representatives who visit Earth in the year 1977. After a period of careful investigation, Sma argues — in the official report that constitutes most of the story — that the Culture needs to intervene to clean up the mess that human beings are making of our world. Were such an intervention to take place, Special Circumstances would spearhead it. However, one of her colleagues finds the  Star Trek television series almost the only redeeming feature of Earth civilization and recommends that the whole planet be destroyed. Special Circumstances would handle that as well.
 The role of Special Circumstances, then, is to implement the Culture Minds’ decisions about how to handle other sentient beings, and those decisions are shaped overwhelmingly by a single criterion: How close is a given civilization to the values and priorities of the Culture itself? If it is intransigently opposed to those values and priorities then perhaps it had best be destroyed before it causes significant mischief — which is what happens to the Idirans, a civilization whose war with the Culture is the primary context for the first Culture novel,    Consider Phlebas (1987). Here we have a conflict in which all wings of Contact play a role because of the scale of the problem. In order to prevent this fanatically religious society from imposing its repressive ideals on other parts of the cosmos, the Culture virtually destroys the whole Idiran culture and a great deal else, including fifty-three planets and half a dozen stars.
 But that is the worst-case scenario. If a civilization reached by Contact contains even a few hopeful seeds, then that world can become a candidate for  mentoring — whether it likes it or not.
 Mentoring — in this frankly, bluntly paternalistic version — is the primary theme of    Matter (2008), the most recent Culture novel. It is not one of Banks’s better efforts, largely because it is overloaded with personnel, but it is built on the wonderful conceit that the civilization being Contacted is more or less like that of  The Lord of the Rings or George R. R. Martin’s A Song of Ice and Fire series: what we would call a “medieval” warrior society. The contrast between these fierce men (and sometimes fiercer women) and the urbane hyper-technologized Culture agents is a delightful one. But, while the plot hinges on the possibility that the whole world will be destroyed, if it does survive there’s really no question about what will happen to the local civilization: it will become Cultured. When the Culture decides to mentor you you will be mentored quite thoroughly, even (or especially) if you’re the kind of society that fans of epic fantasy nostalgically tend to long for.  So Much for Subtlety indeed.
 So it turns out that the closest analogue we have to the Culture’s foreign policy is that of the United States in the recent Bush administration: just as President Bush wanted to spread the good news of American democracy to the rest of the world, and was willing to put some force behind that benevolent imperative, so too the Culture. The Culture is neoconservatism on the greatest imaginable scale. This may seem more than a bit self-contradictory on Banks’s part, given his politics: as he writes, digressively, in his book on whiskey,
 I look at Dubya and just see…a grotesquely under-qualified-for-practically-anything daddy’s boy who’s had to be greased into every squalid position he’s ever held in his miserable existence who might finally be starting to wake up to the idea that if the most powerful nation on Earth — like, ever, dude — can put somebody like him in power, all may not be well with the world.
 But there’s no necessary inconsistency. President Bush — doubtless Banks will love this point — is not a Mind; and the American model of democracy is not that of the Culture. The Culture, Banks seems to think,  deserves to be expanded throughout the known universe, and the Minds can presumably be relied on to manage that expansion in the best possible way.
 But “best possible” is not perfect, because the universe is not perfect — or, more to the point, even the most powerful civilizations within it are deeply flawed. And so when they come within the scope of the Culture’s power, sometimes there’s nothing for the Minds to say but  No More Mister Nice Guy and  Of Course I Still Love You. As yet another Culture ship tells us,  It’s Character Forming. Banks has yet to mention Culture ships called  It’s In Your Own Best Interest or  We’re Doing This For Your Own Good or  This Will Hurt Me More Than It Will Hurt You, but surely they’re out there.
 And maybe all this is true. Sometimes paternal figures — paternal cultures, even? — really  do take action that subordinate figures dislike but that are for the best. Given the wisdom of the Culture’s Minds, don’t they, better than any of the rest of us, know what’s in our own best interest? Perhaps. But Banks, for all his warm praise for the Culture, occasionally plants a seed of doubt.
 Consider the Culture’s relations with a civilization called the Chelgrians. (The history is told as part of the backstory to  Look to Windward.) The Chelgrians evolved on their home world from fierce predators and still retain predatory appearance and, to some degree, personality. Their social order was an exceptionally strict caste-based one, and when Contact began clandestinely observing them, its Minds decided that this structure was a near-absolute impediment to the Chelgrians’ “development” — and so Contact surreptitiously intervened to create a movement devoted to wholesale cultural reconstruction along more liberal and egalitarian lines. The results were a civil war in which billions of Chelgrians died, which in turn led the Culture to own up to its behind-the-scenes manipulations.
 Why was the wisdom of the Culture’s Minds not sufficient to foresee this mess? No explanation is given. Indeed, the Minds of Special Circumstances are surprised fairly often in these novels — in  The Player of Games they seem to realize from the start that they don’t have the political situation on Azad figured out. There are only two inferences I can make here: either Banks is being careless or he is suggesting that even an intelligence capable of handling the everyday affairs of an Orbital containing thirty billion people is  still not smart enough to figure out what sentient beings will do in response to conflict. One hopes the latter is the right inference; but if it is, it suggests that the power of the Minds is largely the power of control: they can predict and deal successfully with the behavior of those who speak their language and use their drugs, but have limited ability to manipulate others.
 And there’s a still more disturbing event at the end of  Look to Windward. The most repulsive character in that book is a Chelgrian, a kind of hit man working for the Chelgrian opposition to the Culture. At the end of the book the Culture sends its own assassin — an artificial but at least partly senti

title: Iain M. Banks的暧昧乌托邦（2009年）
title_en: The Ambiguous Utopia of Iain M. Banks (2009)

note:
适可而止。在政治频谱上，我们美国人正在失去在我们完全复杂的人类中看到彼此的能力。在一个智力束缚和临界思维的国家狂欢中，我们互相减少对不人道的讽刺。这种反人主主义都是通过我们生活在紧急状态的看法，由政治对手 - 南边的敌人造成的感知来造成并持续的是，敌人 - 目的是结束美国实验的意图。关于一个人的对手的好奇心 - 关于他们如何考虑或看世界 - 已经似乎是有罪的。学术人文学科，这比任何其他机构都可能会援助，援助对人文主义和严格的公民奖学金的全面辩护，而是将这种下降加速到反党主义中。这些美德堕落的道德荒谬的荒唐是进一步推动美国人，进一步走向极端，因为一个极端的吸引力而不是因为另一个极端的排斥。
 我们如何从这个困境中提取自己？我们避免了左边的批评，从左边的左右，但在意识形态或政治运动中的批评更加难以找到。缺乏挑战自己的挑战，美国人将继续走向极端，因为中间缺乏更诚实的选择。重建该中心需要对自由主义民主，人文，学术原则的积极承诺与所有人捍卫他们的所有人，无论他们的政治如何。
 对我来说，最好定义这个重要中心的这两种文本是John Donne的“冥想XVII”和Martin Luther King的“来自伯明翰监狱的信”。特别是前者似乎对自己的无神论者来说似乎是一个奇怪的选择，结束了上帝“是我们唯一的安全性”的声明。但我们也可以从基督教人文主义者学习。当Donne写下“上帝”时，我读了他们绝对形式的真理，美丽和正义;当他谈到来世的图书馆时，“每本书都会互相露出，”我听到了这个人的理想。
 Donne的文章是我所知道的最人性化的工作，因为它不妥协坚持我们的互联性：“没有人是一个岛屿，整个自己;每个人都是大陆的一块主要的。“这种相互连接的锚定论文中最着名的线条：当钟声为垂死的人收费时，“永远不会发送钟声响起的人;它为你收费了。“ Donne没有对这些陈述的任何条件或例外。任何男人的死都会减少我，因为我参与了人类。虽然另一个人可能听不到贝尔为他收费，但它有利于我，“如果通过这种危险的考虑，我将自己带入沉思。”没有人死亡是欣喜或贪婪的场合;每个人的死都是一个人们不会寻找罪恶的场合，而是在自己寻找它。
 国王在自由主义民主中调整了Donne的人文主义。他在与唐纳相同的人类相互连接的道德地面：“我们陷入了不可避免的相互关系网络，在一个命运的一件衣服中捆绑在一起。无论什么影响一个人直接影响全部影响。“这种共同的人性可以为他最着名的线条提供逻辑：“任何地方的不公正是对任何地方的威胁。”
 国王也以重要的尊重离开唐尼。 Donne对神圣绝对的感觉使我们自己的人类的罪恶变为相对的明确。为了判断我们对他人的罪恶是为了冒着骄傲的罪，因为我们没有听取钟声。国王了解这种危险，但他仍然尊重社会进步所必需的判断。 “人类进步永远不会在不可避免的轮子上滚动，”他宣称。 “这介绍了男人愿意与上帝同事的不懈努力。”
 在他的来信中，国王区分了越来越少的罪行，越来越少。他正试图找到一个道德中心，他向白克莱梅森解释为批评他作为“极端主义”。 “我站在两个对立力量的中间在黑人社区，”他告诉他们。 “一个是自满的力量，”和其他力量是苦涩和仇恨之一。“ 
幸运的是，这两个对立之间存在的是一个中间人的替代品：“更优秀的爱和非暴力抗议方式。”国王是一种积极的适度，无法区分从“爱的极端主义”。他用一种消极的适度对比这种极端主义对比。 “我几乎达到了令人遗憾的结论，即黑人在步伐中的巨大绊脚石到自由，不是白人公民委员或ku klux klanner，”他写道“，但白温度，谁更加奉献给”订单“司法;谁更喜欢一个负面的和平，这是对存在正义的积极和平的张力。“从最后的判决中萎缩了关于白温（注意到“差不多”），国王致力于必要性，即使在我们的堕落状态，也可以在道德上更好地判断更好的行动课程之间。在自由主义的民主中，他在被压迫和压迫者的共同人类的情况下，非暴力的终结，为道德判断提供了更好的方法。
  Donne的华丽的人文主义和国王的爱心的中锋提供了美国的治疗方法。疾病，明确，不是部落的。部落归属感是一个基本的人类敦促。这种疾病，相当，是脱储极化：越来越多的爱，甚至生命维持的部落债券，越过政治中心，也不是基于政治 - 婚姻，友谊，在战斗中伪造的兄弟会 - 以及新部落的形成基于政治的政治政治领域，并将与其他部落的联合国视为道德污染。决心保持良好的道德优势感，这些部落在杆子误解了自我审查的尝试，作为与对手的道德等效的论据。它们不仅让自己进入道德灾难，而且在这个过程中将国家拉开。 Donne和King的原则使光线远离政治部落的道路，并走向政治中心的部落联合会：疲惫不堪的多数。
  我是一个终身民主党，在政治左边一直觉得大多数人都觉得大多数人。从特朗普的候选人的开始，我有很多原因，我的令人沮丧地厌恶，害怕他，但两个尤为重要。一个人特朗普表现出没有同情容的迹象，因为通过别人的眼睛看到世界。另一个是，特朗普在我们国家的历史中深深地植根了脱奢的黑暗和危险的力量，尤其是与比赛有关的人。 Trumpism不仅仅是除去，而且想象的别人的除法化是它的消失点，就像在宽阔而复杂的帆布上的每一笔技巧都会向一个焦点拉动眼睛。因此，通过特朗普支持者对国会大厦的攻击挥舞着白至高无上的象征可能会有震惊的震惊！ - 右边的推动者，它毫不奇怪。这是一个特朗普主义的逻辑结果。
 民主党人在共和党方面蔑视共和党，因为不能站起来胜过特朗普，但也许感受到政治机会，他们表现出一定令人渴望考虑他们是否可能犯同样的罪行。他们应该。对我们民主的物质心脏的攻击在夜间是一个火响铃。左边可能会发现它是否倾听收费钟并检查自己的良心？
 它会发现，生效，左边的特朗德主义喂养了右边的特朗普主义：大量的美国人的除法，他们认为右翼候选人的投票不是因为右侧拉着它们，但由于左推动它们。
 右边的Trumpismism是肯定的，还要喂养左边的特朗普。作为我喜欢的一位朋友，特朗普没有角色，但他非常善于让别人揭示他们的人。就像工作书出来的东西一样，特朗普考验人们的信仰和对原则的承诺。他在道德上是如此避开，他诱惑他人对他们的道德守则来抵抗他的例外 - 只要这次，我们告诉自己，因为这是紧急情况。但紧急情况持续，“只是这一次”一直变得一直都是，直到我们走到迄今为止，我们不能再认识自己。
  民主党人努力工作，避免了解这一秋季的原因。在2016年6月选举后与自我审查进行了简短调情，相反，他们采用了两种良心清洁的叙述。一个人要说他们一直都是正确的：很多美国人真的是种族主义者，性别歧视，同性恋者，而且太愚蠢了解自己的经济自我利益。另一个叙述是俄罗斯干涉将选举提供给特朗普。这两个叙述几乎完全促使对特朗普崛起的任何责任以及消除对自我检查的艰苦工作的任何责任。 
事实上，左边对其规定的价值观的背叛做了很多，以便在右边能够实现特朗普主义。这种背叛的一个方面，而不是在左边讨论的，是民主党的投资，以牺牲物质问题为代价。但更加紧张的背叛，更常见的背叛是民主党人对民主进程的反对和破坏信仰。
 右翼的是正确的，以指向旧的身份政治和新的Wokeness作为这种除法化的主要司机，尽管它们通常忽视历史和道德复杂性的重要色调。左侧的身份政治开始作为一种揭露和批判权利的右侧谴责的身份政治的一种方式。也就是说，左翼试图表明是白色，男性和异性恋的不是违法的人类身份，正确处理它，并且非白色，非男性和非异性恋同样有效。自然而然，左翼活动家通过寻求将其他身份作为正常和良好的挑战，挑战右翼身份政治的不公正。
 显而易见，很难想象他们如何在没有这样做的情况下成功解放。但这些策略的问题 - 这是右翼批评的地方 - 是他们已经采取了自己的生活，背叛了原始的人文意图。以任何形式的身份政治提出基本化人类类别的危险趋势已成为占主导地位。醒来的醒来现在与授权授权的权利保持速度，并强调他们的陈述负担。实际上，它采用了相同的减少人类类别，从而最初的身份政治追求解放。在这样做时，醒来的醒来的摆锤太远了，远离Unum，朝向百分比，威胁要使我们分成原子并破坏我们的分子键。现代Wokeseness的自我击败逻辑是它的基本表现性质。有这样的东西是实质性的，但它是一个叫做自由主义的老传统。
 与他们寻求帮助的人的同时，醒来的醒来也使他们寻求反对的人，具有破坏性的政治后果。唤醒民主党人非常平息特朗普支持者的复杂性。许多人似乎认为自己的联盟​​比权利更庞大，但右翼联盟是庞大的，左翼的联盟是左边的。特朗普的支持者范围从新纳粹到商会到商会，到钢铁工商到亚裔美国人到古巴难民和超越。他们在我们的武装部队中致力于我们的食物，让我们的火灾，救助我们的动物，拯救我们孩子的生活。虽然标签肯定适合其中一些，但召集所有这些特朗普支持者“白人至上主义者”抢劫任何真正含义的谓词。
 在主管政治战术医生会看到和利用一个不确定系统种族主义的KU KLUX KLAN会员和郊区足球妈妈之间的区别世界之中，醒来左派看到了一个白色的最高医生和白色的最精致的等待。而不是试图在三个维度中看到，甚至两个甚至两个，醒来的左转已经将特朗普主义画布折叠成其消失点。它无法看到其对手的完整，复杂的人性是其导致和效果无法看到它想要帮助的人的完整，复杂的人性。
  除了左侧的人文主义背叛之外，共和党人还正确地指出了与民主进程的不耐烦。常常，民主党人试图通过转向法院或技术专区来分配群众的辛勤工作。对于群众来说，群众才能推断民主党人发现它们的兴奋是自然的。
 由于左转转向法院，别处覆盖了别处，我将专注于其对技术专家的偏好。民主党谈论气候变化的方式提供了依赖于民主进程的专家捷径的良好榜样。随着民主党坚持的，气候是否正在发生变化的问题以及人类是否导致变革是正确的科学问题。这就是为什么我接受科学共识，即两个问题的答案是肯定的。但是，对人类诱发的气候变化做些什么不是科学问题;这是一个政治之一，因为我们选择的任何反应都会对资源分布产生影响，这是政治的本质。要修改普鲁士军事哲学家Carl Von Clasewitz的着名线路，可能会说转向可再生能源是通过其他方式继续进行政策。 
当涉及民主中的政治问题时，人民是主权，而不是专家。民主党人无法区分科学和政治问题，对他们对民主的理解表示怀疑。要清楚，我并不倡导放弃专业知识的概念或建议我们不应该听专家，而不是我认为我们应该忽视将军和海军上将在战争决定时。但我们的民主在军队中有一个平民至上的传统，这是一项原因，这是战争是通过其他方式进行政策的延续。事实上，我们国家民事化关系框架更广泛为公民关系提供了框架，但这不是民主党人使用的框架。
 复合民主党人对专业知识的政治影响是，专家自己经常破坏自己对专业知识的索赔，并与其政策建议的不利影响不成比例地绝缘。当专家未能认识到自己的专业知识的限制 - 例如，通过使他们的专家能力陷入困境的问题，因为气候科学家经常会更广泛地侵蚀美国人对专业知识的信仰。与此同时，专家要求对其建议影响的大多数美国人不可用的经济安全程度。当流行病学家建议锁定减缓Covid-19的传播时，他们冒着别人的工作，而不是他们自己的冒险。这是一场小幼苗的变体 - 我的后院没有风险。他们在游戏中缺乏皮肤让他们在努力从后方领导的道德地位 - 就像美国精英一样，随着逃避军事服务，自越南以来一直在努力做。为什么美国人愿意冒着专家不驾驶自己的风险？
 右翼Trumpism的另一个主要司机是左侧的无法为美国历史上的经验准确和政治发作版本提供。左边有这个批评版的版本和右边。矿井这样的跑步：左边是思想主义上的一体压迫，而对美国过去的一切诠释，主要是因为担心重新刻上早期的压迫。 （纽约时报的1619项目是这一承诺的一个很好的例子。）根据盛行的左翼解释，美国成立为种族主义，性别歧视，同性恋国家，它一直保持如此。
 这种解释是经验不准确的，因为它忽略了随着时间的推移变化的证据，并使美国过去的多样性变平。随着时间的变化而变化已经削减了两种方式。例如，富有成效物业的所有权现在比在内战前更集中，但黑人的法律奴役是相当不那么常见的。它肯定是真的，在成立时存在白人至高无上。但是，在成立时，对白色至上和家人主义的反对也是如此。所有这些不同的电流都是美国作为苹果派。
 此外，盛行的左翼解释不仅是不准确的;它也在政治上是自我挫败的。我不是第一个值得注意的是，“反种族主义者”解释美国的创始人与白人至高无上的解释有很大的共同之处：Neo-Nazis就像醒来的活动家声称美国成立的那样渴望作为一个白人的国家，尽管有意识形态的原因。全压制，美国历史的全时解释为激进政治提供了基础，因为它不提供这一时间将是任何不同的。
 作为批评者在右边和左上方的左侧已经指出，很难想象左边的身份政治，厌恶政治过程的结合如何，对美国历史的扭曲理解可以持有民主国家。如果我们主要通过我们的种族，性别和性行为与世界互动，我们如何将其作为全面的美国人互动？如果我们转向专家来解决我们的痛苦分歧，我们是如何一种民主的？如果我们的历史都是压迫，一直都是为什么我们想要成为美国人，为什么我们希望为未来的选择？民主党人无法为这些问题提供令人信服的答案 - 这反映了左派的特朗普主义 - 代表他们对右边的特朗普主义增长的签名贡献。
  虽然民主党人的核心原则背叛了特朗普，但它在他下面加速了。 
我们可以先看到这一点，也许是最明白的，在特朗普支持者视为抵抗的原始罪中：俄罗斯调查。在钉子特朗普的全面努力的过程中，民主党人在伤害他们的身边时，谴责联邦调查局的政治化，同时欢迎它在使其受益时。当它违反时，他们在逃避时宣布了法治的神圣性。 Carter页面的治疗，一个特朗普宣传助手，呈现出一个很好的例子。
  在与俄罗斯的特朗普竞选联系人启动其探测后不久，联邦调查局向FAGE开设了一个案例。一周后，中央情报局通知联邦调查局通过与俄罗斯官员的谈话报告，该页面已作为原子能机构的来源。但是，当FBI提交其第一个FISA认股权证申请时，它省略了任何提及页面的挖掘性CIA Connection。更糟糕的是，在随后的保证申请中，联邦调查局律师从中央情报局官员改变了一封电子邮件，这似乎看起来虽然页面没有为本机构工作。联邦调查局还使用了以此以自标记的斯蒂尔·档案档案，以支持页面上的监测应用。甚至更具可疑的是，FBI基于斯蒂尔的信息作为档案可靠性的证据。实际上，联邦调查局抑制了和误用的证据

Enough is enough. Across the political spectrum, we Americans are losing the ability to see one another in our full complex humanity. In  a national orgy of intellectual incuriosity and uncritical thinking, we’re reducing each other to inhuman caricatures. This anti-humanism both creates and is sustained by the perception that we’re living in a state of emergency, confronted by political opponents—nay, enemies—intent on ending the American experiment. Curiosity about one’s adversaries—about how they think or see the world—has come to seem sinful. The academic humanities, which more than any other institution might have come to our aid with a full-throated defense of humanism and rigorous civic scholarship, have instead accelerated this descent into anti-humanism. The moral disrepute into which these virtues have fallen is driving Americans further and further toward the extremes—less because the one extreme attracts than because the other extreme repels.
 How do we extract ourselves from this predicament? We are awash in critiques of the Left from the Right, and of the Right from the Left, but critiques from within an ideology or political movement are harder to find. Absent a greater willingness to challenge one’s own side, Americans will continue to move to the extremes, for lack of a more honest option in the middle. Rebuilding the center requires a positive commitment to liberal-democratic, humanistic, scholarly principles, in alliance with all who defend them, whatever their politics.
 For me, the two texts that best define this vital center are  John Donne’s “ Meditation XVII” and Martin Luther King’s “ Letter from Birmingham Jail.” The former in particular may seem an odd choice for an atheist like myself, ending as it does with the statement that God “is our only security.” But we secular humanists can learn from Christian humanists, too. When Donne writes “God,” I read Truth, Beauty, and Justice in their absolute form; when he speaks of the library of the afterlife, “where every book shall lie open to one another,” I hear the humanistic ideal in this one.
 Donne’s essay is the most humane work I know, for its uncompromising insistence on our interconnectedness: “No man is an island, entire of himself; every man is a piece of the continent, a part of the main.” This interconnectedness anchors the most famous line in the essay: when a bell tolls for a dying man, “never send to know for whom the bell tolls; it tolls for thee.” Donne offers no conditions or exceptions to these statements.  Any man’s death diminishes me, because I am involved in mankind. Though another may not hear the bell tolling for him, it benefits me, “if by this consideration of another’s danger, I take mine own into contemplation.” No one’s death is an occasion for rejoicing or gloating; everyone’s death is an occasion not to search others for sin, but to search for it in oneself.
 King adapts Donne’s humanism to the world of practical politics in a liberal democracy. He grounds his ethics on the same human interconnectedness as Donne: “We are caught in an inescapable network of mutuality, tied in a single garment of destiny. Whatever affects one directly affects all indirectly.” This shared humanity supplies the logic for perhaps his most famous line: “Injustice anywhere is a threat to justice everywhere.”
 King also departs from Donne in important respects. Donne’s sense of the divine absolute flattens our own human sins into relative sameness. To judge our sins against others’ is to risk the sin of pride, because we aren’t listening to the tolling bell. King understands this danger, but he nevertheless regards judgment to be necessary for social advance. “Human progress never rolls in on wheels of inevitability,” he declares. “It comes through the tireless efforts of men willing to be co-workers with God.”
 Throughout his letter, King distinguishes between more and less sinful actions, more and less just actions. He is trying to find a moral center, he explains to the white clergymen who had criticized him as an “extremist.” “I stand in the middle of two opposing forces in the Negro community,” he tells them. “One is a force of complacency,” and “[t]he other force is one of bitterness and hatred.”
 Fortunately, there exists between these two opposing forces a centrist alternative: “the more excellent way of love and nonviolent protest.” King’s is a positive moderation, indistinguishable from an “extremism for love.” He contrasts this extremism with a type of negative moderation. “I have almost reached the regrettable conclusion that the Negro’s great stumbling block in the stride toward freedom is not the White Citizens Counciler or the Ku Klux Klanner,” he writes, “but the white moderate, who is more devoted to ‘order’ than to justice; who prefers a negative peace which is the absence of tension to a positive peace which is the presence of justice.” Shrinking from a final judgment about the white moderate (note the “almost”), King nevertheless commits to the necessity, even in our fallen state, of doing our best to judge between morally better and morally worse courses of action. In a liberal democracy, he concludes, nonviolence, premised on the shared humanity of oppressed and oppressor, offers a better way to act on moral judgments.
  Donne’s gorgeous humanism and King’s loving centrism offer the cure to what ails the United States. The disease, to be clear, isn’t tribalism. A sense of tribal belonging is a fundamental human urge. The disease, rather, is dehumanizing polarization:  the severing of often loving, even life-sustaining tribal bonds that cross the political center and aren’t based on politics—marriages, friendships, brotherhoods forged in combat—and the formation of new tribes at the political poles, which are based on politics and regard confederation with other tribes as morally polluting. Determined to preserve a sense of their moral superiority, these tribes at the poles misinterpret attempts at self-scrutiny as arguments for moral equivalence with their opponents. They are not only driving themselves into moral disaster but pulling the country apart in the process. The principles that Donne and King espoused light the path away from the political tribes at the poles and toward the tribal confederation of the political center: the  exhausted majority.
  I’m a lifelong Democrat who has always felt most at home on the political left. From the beginnings of Trump’s candidacy, I have viscerally loathed and feared him for many reasons, but two are especially important. One is that Trump shows no sign of a capacity for empathy, for seeing the world through others’ eyes. The other is that Trump plays with dark and dangerous forces of dehumanization that are deeply rooted in our nation’s history, especially ones related to race. Trumpism isn’t only dehumanization, but the dehumanization of an imagined other is its vanishing point, just as every brushstroke on a broad and complex canvas pulls the eyes toward a single focal point. Thus, however much the attack on the Capitol by Trump supporters brandishing symbols of white supremacy may have shocked—shocked!—his enablers on the right, it came as no surprise. It was a logical consequence of Trumpism.
 Democrats have rightly heaped scorn on the Republican Party for failing to stand up to Trump, but, perhaps sensing political opportunity, they have shown somewhat less eagerness to consider whether they might be committing the same sins. They should. The attack on the physical heart of our democracy was a fire bell in the night. What might the Left find if it listens to the tolling bell and examines its own conscience?
 It would find, in effect,  a Trumpism of the left which feeds the Trumpism of the right: a dehumanization of large swaths of Americans who feel impelled to vote for right-wing candidates not because the Right pulls them but because the Left pushes them.
 Trumpism on the right, to be sure, also feeds the Trumpism of the left. As a friend of mine likes to say, Trump has no character, but he’s very good at getting others to reveal theirs. Like something out of the Book of Job, Trump tests people’s faith and commitment to principles. He is so morally repellent that he tempts others to make exceptions to their moral codes to resist him—just this once, we tell ourselves, because this is an emergency. But the emergency lasts, and “just this once” becomes all the time, until we’ve fallen so far that we can no longer recognize ourselves.
  Democrats have worked hard to avoid understanding the reasons for this fall. After a brief flirtation with self-scrutiny in the wake of the 2016 election, they instead adopted two conscience-cleansing narratives. One was to say that they had been right all along: lots of Americans really are racist, sexist, homophobic, and too stupid to understand their own economic self-interest. The other narrative was that Russian interference delivered the election to Trump. These two narratives were almost perfectly primed to absolve the Left of any responsibility for Trump’s rise and to eliminate any need for the hard work of self-examination.
 In fact, the Left’s betrayal of its stated values did a great deal to enable Trumpism on the right. One aspect of this betrayal, not infrequently discussed on the left, is the Democrats’ investment of too much energy into cultural issues at the expense of material issues. But an even more consequential betrayal, much more commonly noted on the right, is that Democrats have dehumanized their opponents and broken faith with the democratic process.
 Right-wingers are correct to point to the old identity politics and the new wokeness as the principal drivers of this dehumanization, though they typically ignore important shades of historical and moral complexity. Identity politics on the left began as a way to expose and critique the unacknowledged identity politics of the right. That is, the Left sought to show that being white, male, and heterosexual was not the default human identity that the Right treated it as, and that being non-white, non-male, and non-heterosexual were equally valid. Naturally, left-wing activists challenged the injustice of right-wing identity politics by seeking to redefine other identities as normal and good.
 Tactically, it’s hard to imagine how they could have succeeded at emancipation without doing so. But the problem with these tactics—and here is where the right-wing critique comes in—is that they have taken on a life of their own and betrayed their original humanistic intentions. A dangerous tendency to essentialize categories of humanity, present in any form of identity politics, has become dominant. The woke Left now keeps pace with the Right in tokenizing minorities and imposing on them the burden of representation. In effect, it’s adopted the same reductionist categories of humanity from which identity politics originally sought emancipation.  In so doing,  the woke Left has swung the pendulum much too far away from the  unum and toward the  pluribus, threatening to fragment us into atoms  and destroy our molecular bonds.  The self-defeating logic of modern wokeness is what gives it its essentially performative quality. There is such a thing as substantive wokeness, but it’s an older tradition called liberalism.
 At the same time as they’ve dehumanized those they seek to help, the woke Left has also dehumanized those they seek to oppose, with devastating political consequences. Woke Democrats terribly flatten the complexity of Trump supporters. Many seem to think that their own coalition is more sprawling and more diverse than the Right’s, but the right-wing coalition is every bit as sprawling and diverse as the Left’s. Trump’s supporters range from neo-Nazis to Chamber of Commerce conservatives to steelworkers to Asian-Americans to Cuban refugees and beyond. They grow our food, serve in our armed forces, fight our fires, rescue our animals, and save the lives of our children. Though the label certainly fits some, to call all these Trump supporters “white supremacists” robs the appellation of any real meaning.
 Where a competent political tactician would see and exploit the world of difference between a Ku Klux Klan member and a suburban soccer mom who isn’t sure systemic racism is a thing, the woke Left sees a white supremacist and a white supremacist-in-waiting. Instead of trying to see in three dimensions, or even two, the woke Left has collapsed the Trumpist canvas into its vanishing point. Its inability to see the full, complex humanity of its opponents is both cause and effect of its inability to see the full, complex humanity of those it wants most to help.
  In addition to the Left’s betrayal of humanism, Republicans also rightly point to its impatience with the democratic process. All too often, Democrats try to dispense with the hard work of persuading the masses by turning to the courts or to technocrats. It’s only natural for the masses to infer that the Democrats find them revolting.
 Since the Left’s turn to the courts has been  ably covered elsewhere, I’ll focus on its preference for technocrats. The way the Democrats talk about climate change offers a good example of their reliance on expert shortcuts to the democratic process. As Democrats insist, the questions of whether the climate is changing and whether human beings are causing the change are properly scientific questions. That’s why I accept the scientific consensus that the answer to both questions is yes. But the question of what to do about human-induced climate change is not a scientific question; it’s a political one, because whatever response we choose will have implications for resource distribution, which is the very essence of politics. To modify a famous line by the Prussian military philosopher Carl von Clausewitz, it might be said that switching to renewable energy is the continuation of policy by other means.
  When it comes to political questions in a democracy, the people are sovereign, not the experts. Democrats’ inability to distinguish between scientific and political questions casts doubt on their understanding of democracy. To be clear, I’m not advocating the abandonment of the concept of expertise or suggesting that we shouldn’t listen to experts, any more than I’d argue we should ignore generals and admirals when we make decisions about war. But our democracy has a tradition of civilian supremacy over the military for a reason, which is that war is a continuation of policy by other means. Indeed, our nation’s framework for civil-military relations offers a framework for civil-expert relations more broadly, but it’s not the framework that Democrats use.
 Compounding the political effects of the Democrats’ reliance on expertise is the fact that the experts themselves frequently undermine their own claims to expertise and are disproportionately insulated from the adverse effects of their policy advice. When experts fail to recognize the limits of their own expertise—for instance by confusing questions that fall within their expert competence with those that don’t, as climate scientists often do—they corrode Americans’ faith in expertise more broadly. At the same time, experts demand a level of economic security that is unavailable to most of the Americans whom their advice affects. When epidemiologists recommended lockdowns to slow the spread of covid-19, they were risking other people’s jobs, not their own. It’s a variant of NIMBYism—no  risk in my back yard. Their lack of skin in the game puts them in the moral position of trying to lead from the rear—just as American elites, with their avoidance of military service, have been trying to do since Vietnam. Why should Americans be willing to run risks that experts don’t run themselves?
 Another major driver of right-wing Trumpism is the Left’s inability to offer Americans an empirically accurate and politically emancipatory version of U.S. history. There are versions of this critique on the Left as well as the Right. Mine runs like this: The Left is ideologically committed to an all-oppression, all-the-time interpretation of the American past, largely for fear of re-inscribing earlier oppression. (The  New York Times’ 1619 Project is a good example of this commitment.) According to the prevailing left-wing interpretation, America was founded as a racist, sexist, homophobic nation, and it has always remained so.
 This interpretation is empirically inaccurate because it ignores evidence of change over time and flattens the diversity of the American past. Change over time has cut both ways. For instance, ownership of productive property is more concentrated now than it was before the Civil War, but the legal enslavement of black people is rather less common. It’s certainly true that white supremacy and paternalism existed at the Founding. But it’s also true that  opposition to white supremacy and paternalism was present at the Founding. All of these different currents are as American as apple pie.
 Furthermore, the prevailing left-wing interpretation isn’t just inaccurate; it’s also politically self-defeating. I’m not the first to note that the “anti-racist” interpretation of the American Founding has a great deal in common with the white supremacist interpretation: neo-Nazis are just as eager as woke activists to claim that the United States was  founded as a white man’s nation, albeit for ideologically opposite reasons. The all-oppression, all-the-time interpretation of U.S. history provides no grounding for radical politics, because it provides no hope that this time will be any different.
 As critics on the right and increasingly on the left have  pointed  out, it’s difficult to imagine how the Left’s combination of woke identity politics, aversion to the political process, and warped understanding of U.S. history could hold a democratic nation together. If we engage with the world primarily through our race, gender, and sexuality, how can we engage with it as fully human Americans? If we turn to experts to solve our bitterest disagreements, how are we a democracy? If our history is all oppression, all the time, why do we want to be American, and why should we hope for a better future? The Democrats’ inability to offer compelling answers to these questions—which reflects the Trumpism of the left—represents their signature contribution to the growth of the Trumpism of the right.
  While the Democrats’ self-defeating betrayal of core principles predates Trump, it has accelerated under him.
 We can see this first, and perhaps most plainly, in what Trump supporters regard as the original sin of the Resistance: the Russia investigation. In the course of their all-out effort to nail Trump, the Democrats decried the politicization of the FBI when it hurt their side, while welcoming it when it benefited them. And they proclaimed the sanctity of the rule of law while looking away when it was violated. The treatment of Carter Page, a Trump campaign aide, presents an excellent example.
  Shortly after initiating its probe into the Trump campaign’s contacts with Russia, the FBI opened a case against Page. A week later, the CIA informed the FBI that Page had worked as a source for the agency by reporting on his conversations with Russian officials. When the FBI submitted its first FISA warrant application, however, it omitted any mention of Page’s exculpatory CIA connection. Worse, in a subsequent warrant application, an FBI lawyer altered an email from a CIA official, making it seem as though Page had not worked for the agency. The FBI also  used the since-discredited Steele dossier to support its surveillance applications on Page. Even more questionable was the FBI’s  tautological invocation of news reports based on information from Steele as evidence of the dossier’s reliability. In effect, the FBI suppressed and misused evidence to go after P

    剖宫产后的感染特别捕获泰勒的注意力。在一些非洲国家，高达20％的妇女患有C型患者，然后发展手术部位感染。研究还表明，刚果民主共和国和布隆迪的塞拉利昂的健康中心具有相似或更低的感染率，在C-Section之后的2％和10％之间，速度范围为8％至10％ 。
  但智能手机访问明显不同。 2016年发布的BBC调查发现，在塞拉利昂，约有53％的人拥有手机，大约四分之三的这些拥有的基本手机，而不是智能手机。
  “我在我的社区中完成了很多种族股权工作，我是几个会议的访客，”泰勒说。 “所以，当我有这个机会进行研究时，我无法帮助，但是用股票镜头进行帮助。”
  在Covid-19 Pandemer之前，泰勒在Black历史博览会上放学后的大部分时间，她是自八年级以来的俱乐部，并参加每周学校董事会和地区会议，以倡导反种族主义课程。在2020年2月20日的第一个区域科学博览会上的四个月，泰勒在周五下午犯下了在她的化学老师，卡罗琳迎来的指导下研究。 
健康的人体皮肤自然是酸性的，pH左右。但是当伤口被感染时，它的pH值达到约九。没有电子器件可以检测到pH的变化;许多水果和蔬菜是在不同pH水平下改变颜色的天然指标。
  “我发现甜菜改变了完美的pH点的颜色，”泰勒说。明亮的红色甜菜汁在九点的pH下变为黑暗的紫色。 “那个＆＃39;非常适合受感染的伤口。所以，我就像，'哦，好吧。所以甜菜是它的地方＆＃39;“

  棉涤纶混合物检查了所有盒子。在感染的pH值下五分钟后，棉涤纶线从鲜红色变为深紫色。三天后，紫色淡入浅灰色。
      她希望在全球健康中关注股权，希望有一天会有一些变色的缝合线可以帮助患者尽早检测手术部位感染，以便在它具有最大的影响时可以寻求医疗保健。泰勒计划专利她的发明。与此同时，她正在等待她的最终大学招生结果。
  “为了到达前40名，这就像这些孩子正在做的博士后工作，”科学协会总裁兼首席执行官Maya Ajmera表示，经营科学人才搜索。今年的顶级奖项前往匹配算法，可以在无限的选项中找到对的成对，这是一个有助于识别药物研究的有用化合物和可持续饮用水过滤系统的计算机模型。决赛选手还投票给了泰勒的Seaborg奖，为她的队列制作了一位发言人。
  南非斯泰伦博斯大学全球外科大学全球外科院长Kathryn Chu侧重于改善公平进入外科护理。 “我认为这是一个惊人的，这个年轻的高中科学家灵感在解决解决这个问题的解决方案中，”外科医生在一封电子邮件中写道。 “可以检测到早期[手术部位感染]的产品将是非常有价值的。” 
然而，她补充说，“这个概念如何从替补席到床边需要进一步测试。”
  目前的缝线线件擅长他们的工作：他们实惠，他们对皮肤没有刺激，它们足以举起伤口。甜菜果汁染色线程需要对所有这些属性具有竞争力。手术部位感染也可能发生在伤口表面以下，涉及切割，然后修复，而不仅仅是皮肤，而且还有肌肉。当它所处时，色彩改变的缝线线不会有助于检测皮肤下方的感染，并且如果感染通过皮肤渗出，或涉及皮肤，感染已经达到了以后阶段，“楚都达到了阶段。”
  最后，用甜菜汁难以染色的标准缝线螺纹的相同的不吸收性也使细菌脱落，反之亦然。虽然棉线的编织结构使其能够拿起甜菜染料，但它还为引起感染的细菌提供了一个隐藏的地方。
  自从她的项目开始以来，泰勒一直在追求一系列研究，可能会抵消使用棉花所带来的风险。
  “我读过一些关于甜菜汁是抗菌的研究。虽然我想拿他们的话，但我想为自己试试。我想重现他们的结果，“泰勒说。
    但是研究细菌需要特定的无菌实践，泰勒和她的导师都不是化学教师，也不是化学教师，也不熟悉。在通往科学人才搜索竞赛的几个月里，爱荷华大学与微生物学家Ho相关的泰勒建立了一个采用适当技术的研究计划，并正在进行工作。
  反思科学公平经历，泰勒最想感谢Carolyn Wall的鼓励她参加。 “我们＆＃39;在所有这些中都在一起晒太阳，”她说，特别是因为这是她第一年进行独立研究。她还感谢她的社区的支持。 
“我有这么多的学校骄傲，因为当我们学校的某个人做了很大的事情时，他们会尽可能地庆祝，”泰勒说。 “能够成为其中一个孩子这么神奇。”  毕业后，泰勒希望参加霍华德大学，学习政治学，最终成为律师。  “我期待看到Dasia如何使用这个项目前进，”Ajmera说。 “并且在长期规模上，我真的很兴趣观看她要继续解决的问题，让世界变得更美好。”
note_en:
Dasia Taylor has juiced about three dozen beets in the last 18 months. The root vegetables, she’s found, provide the perfect dye for her invention: suture thread that changes color, from bright red to dark purple, when a surgical wound becomes infected.
    The 17-year-old student at Iowa City West High School in Iowa City, Iowa, began working on the project in October 2019, after her chemistry teacher shared information about state-wide science fairs with the class. As she developed her sutures, she nabbed awards at  several regional science fairs, before advancing to the national stage. This January, Taylor was named one of 40 finalists in the  Regeneron Science Talent Search, the country’s oldest and most prestigious science and math competition for high school seniors.
  As any science fair veteran knows, at the core of a successful project is a problem in need of solving. Taylor had read about sutures coated with a conductive material that can sense the status of a wound by changes in electrical resistance, and relay that information to the smartphones or computers of patients and doctors. While these “smart” sutures could help in the United States, the expensive tool might be less applicable to people in developing countries, where internet access and mobile technology is sometimes lacking. And yet the need is there; on average, 11 percent of surgical wounds develop an infection in low- and middle-incoming countries, according to the  World Health Organization, compared to between  2 and 4 percent of surgeries in the U.S.
    Infections after Cesarean sections particularly caught Taylor’s attention. In some African nations, up to  20 percent of women who give birth by C-section then develop surgical site infections.  Research has also shown that health centers in Sierra Leone, the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Burundi have similar or lower rates of infection, at between 2 and 10 percent, following C-sections than the  U.S., where rates range from 8 to 10 percent.
  But smartphone access is markedly different. A  BBC survey published in 2016 found that in Sierra Leone, about 53 percent of people own mobile phones, and about three-quarters of those owned basic cell phones, not smartphones.
  “I've done a lot of racial equity work in my community, I've been a guest speaker at several conferences,” says Taylor. “So when I was presented with this opportunity to do research, I couldn't help but go at it with an equity lens.”
  Before the Covid-19 pandemic, Taylor spent most of her time after school in the  Black History Game Show, a club she’s been a member of since eighth grade, and attending  weekly school board and district meetings to advocate for an anti-racist curriculum. For the four months leading up to her first regional science fair in February 2020, Taylor committed Friday afternoons to research under the guidance of her chemistry teacher, Carolyn Walling.
  Healthy human skin is naturally  acidic, with a pH around five. But when a wound becomes infected, its pH goes up to about nine. Changes in pH can be detected without electronics; many fruits and vegetables are natural indicators that change color at different pH levels.
  “I found that beets changed color at the perfect pH point,” says Taylor. Bright red beet juice turns dark purple at a pH of nine. “That's perfect for an infected wound. And so, I was like, ‘Oh, okay. So beets is where it's at.’”
  Next, Taylor had to find a suture thread that would hold onto the dye. She tested ten different materials, including standard suture thread, for how well they picked up and held the dye, whether the dye changed color when its pH changed, and how their thickness compared to standard suture thread. After her school transitioned to remote learning, she could spend four or five hours in the lab on an asynchronous lesson day, running experiments.
  A cotton-polyester blend checked all the boxes. After five minutes under an infection-like pH, the cotton-polyester thread changes from bright red to dark purple. After three days, the purple fades to light gray.
      Working with an eye on equity in global health, she hopes that the color-changing sutures will someday help patients detect surgical site infections as early as possible so that they can seek medical care when it has the most impact. Taylor plans to patent her invention. In the meantime, she’s waiting for her final college admissions results.
  “To get to the Top 40, this is like post-doctoral work that these kids are doing,” says  Maya Ajmera, the president and CEO of the Society for Science, which runs the Science Talent Search. This year’s top prizes went to a matching algorithm that can find pairs in an infinite pool of options, a computer model that can help identify useful compounds for pharmaceutical research and a sustainable drinking water filtration system. The finalists also voted to grant Taylor the  Seaborg Award, making her a spokesperson for their cohort.
  Kathryn Chu, the director of the  Center for Global Surgery at Stellenbosch University in South Africa, focuses on improving equitable access to surgical care. “I think it is amazing that this young high school scientist was inspired to work on a solution to address this problem,” the surgeon writes in an email. “A product that could detect early [surgical site infections] would be extremely valuable.”
  However, she adds, “how this concept could translate from the bench to the bedside needs further testing.”
  Current suture threads are good at their job: they’re affordable, they’re not irritating on the skin, and they are strong enough to hold a wound together. The beet juice-dyed thread will need to be competitive on all of these attributes. Surgical site infections can also occur below the surface of a wound—a C-section involves cutting through, and then repairing, not just the skin but also the muscle underneath. As it stands, the color-changing suture thread wouldn’t help detect an infection below the skin, and “if the infection oozes through the skin, or involves the skin, the infection has already reached later stages,” writes Chu.
  Lastly, the same non-absorbency that makes standard suture thread hard to dye with beet juice also keeps bacteria out, and vice versa. While cotton thread’s braided structure gives it the ability to pick up the beet dye, it also provides a hiding place for bacteria that cause infections.
  Taylor has been pursuing a line of research since the beginning of her project that might counteract the risks posed by using cotton.
  “I read some studies that said beet juice was antibacterial. And although I want to take their word, I wanted to try it for myself. I wanted to reproduce their results,” says Taylor.
    But studying bacteria requires specific, sterile practices that neither Taylor, nor her mentors Walling and Michelle Wikner, both chemistry teachers, were initially familiar with. In the months leading up to the Science Talent Search competition, Taylor connected with microbiologist  Theresa Ho at University of Iowa to create a research plan incorporating the proper techniques, and that work is ongoing.
  Reflecting on the science fair experience, Taylor most wants to thank Carolyn Walling for encouraging her to participate. “We're kind of basking in all of this together,” she says, especially because it’s her first year doing independent research. She’s also thankful for the support of her community.
  “I have so much school pride because when somebody in our school does something great, they're celebrated to its fullest extent,” says Taylor. “And being able to be one of those kids has been so amazing.”
  After graduation, Taylor hopes to attend Howard University, study political science and eventually become a lawyer.
  “I am looking forward to seeing how Dasia uses this project moving forward,” says Ajmera. “And on a long-term scale, I’m really interested in watching what problems she is going to continue to solve, to make the world a better place.”

上海（路透社） - 中国在2020年产生了全球总燃煤电力的53％，九个百分点，五年来，尽管气候承诺和数百种可再生能源工厂的建设，全球数据研究于周一显示。
  虽然中国增加了71.7千兆瓦（GW）的风电和去年的48.2 GW，但伦敦能源和气候研究小组有唯一能够看到燃煤发电中的巨大跳跃。 。
 该报告显示，中国煤炭发电量升至1.7％或77特拉瓦特，足以将其占全球煤炭权力的份额为53％，从2015年占44％。
 中国已答应减少对煤炭的依赖，并将气候变暖温室气体排放到2030年之前的峰值，并成为2060年的“碳中性”。
  “中国就像一艘大船，这需要时间才能转向另一个方向，”奥伯和报告的作者之一高级分析师Muyi Yang说。
 中国一直无法找到足够的干净能源，以满足需求的快速增长，可再生能源于去年仅占中国的电力消耗增长的一半。
 根据二月的研究报告，新的燃煤电力装置达到了38.4 GW，超过了世界其他地区建造的三倍以上。 
中国还批准了去年46.1 GW的新燃煤项目，超过前三年，随着新项目仍然得到前进，直到今年下年，环境集团在周一表示。  中国在十年前大约70％的煤炭占据总能源消费的份额，去年的56.8％。 但绝对的一代卷在2016  -  2012年期间上涨了19％，Ember计算出。  在其2021-2025五年计划中，中国发誓要“理性地控制燃煤能力建设中发展的规模和步伐”，杨表示更加艰难的措施。  “我认为将有煤炭消费量，这将对未来煤炭轨迹产生重大影响，”他说。

SHANGHAI (Reuters) - China generated 53% of the world’s total coal-fired power in 2020, nine percentage points more that five years earlier, despite climate pledges and the building of hundreds of renewable energy plants, a global data study showed on Monday.
  Although China added a record 71.7 gigawatts (GW) of wind power and 48.2 GW of solar last year, it was the only G20 nation to see a significant jump in coal-fired generation, said Ember, the London-based energy and climate research group.
 China’s coal-fired generation rose by 1.7% or 77 terawatt-hours, enough to bring its share of global coal power to 53%, up from 44% in 2015, the report showed.
 China has promised to reduce its dependence on coal and bring emissions of climate-warming greenhouse gas to a peak before 2030 and become “carbon neutral” by 2060.
  “China is like a big ship, and it takes time to turn in another direction,” said Muyi Yang, senior analyst with Ember and one of the report’s authors.
 China has been unable to find enough clean energy to meet rapid increases in demand, with renewables meeting only half of China’s power consumption growth last year.
 New coal-fired power installations reached 38.4 GW in 2020, more than three times the amount built by the rest of the world, according to a February research report.
  China also approved 46.1 GW of new coal-fired projects last year, more than the previous three years combined, with new projects still getting the go-ahead until late in the year, environment group Greenpeace said on Monday.
 China slashed the share of coal in total energy consumption from around 70% a decade ago to 56.8% last year. But absolute generation volumes rose 19% over the 2016-2020 period, Ember calculated.
 In its 2021-2025 five-year plan, China vowed to “rationally control the scale and pace of development in the construction of coal-fired power,” and Yang said tougher measures could follow.
 “I think there will be a cap on coal consumption, and that will have a major impact on the future trajectory for coal power,” he said.
title: 中国在2020年产生了超过半世界的燃煤电力
title_en: China generated over half world's coal-fired power in 2020
